coredata.dc:title: Salt-embedded carbon nanodots as a UV and thermal stable fluorophore for light-emitting diodes
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Tak H.
Wang, Fu
McCormick, Paul
Wang, Lianzhou
Brown, Chris
Li, Qin
coredata.dc:description: UV and thermal stable, photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) prepared by embedding CDs in ionic salt crystals such as NaCl, KCl, KBr are demonstrated. The salt crystal embedding matrix does not interfere with CDs strong emission, and provides effective protection to CDs from the environment. The degradation of 20% of the initial luminescence intensity of salt-encapsulated CDs (S-CDs) is 15 times slower under UV and 6 times slower under heat compared to that of CDs in silica matrix. We also demonstrate that the S-CDs can be applied as a color-converting phosphor for typical GaN UV light emitting diodes (LEDs) with significant improvements in stability as well as processability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescent nanoparticles
Carbon nanodots
UV stability
Thermal stability
Color-converting phosphors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of S-CD-LEDs and the stability test
UV stability of solid state CDs
Thermal stability of solid state CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Crystallization of CDs in saturated KBr solution and harvested crystals under natural and UV light (from left to right), (b) and (c) TEM images of S-CDs (CDs in KBr) taken from the exact same areas of the sample (the progression of the shapes of the sample ((b) to (c)) is caused by melting of the ionic crystal upon electron beam exposure), (d) and (e) represent TEM image of S-CDs and their corresponding EDX data.
(a) Steady state emission spectra (λ ex=360nm) and (b) time-resolved (λ ex=375nm) decay curves of the samples. Insets show solution sample under natural and UV light (365nm).

(a) UV stability of CD in silica and S-CDs in NaCl, KCl and KBr salts and (b) lifetime changes before and after UV exposure. (c) Thermal stability of bare CD and S-CD samples over 250h and (d) a close-up plot of thermal stability of all samples in the first 8h.

(a) Spectra and (b) CIE coordination chart of LEDs. Inset shows LED under natural light, bare LED, NaCl S-CD-LED low and high concentrations (clockwise from top left), (c) PL intensity of LED tested for one week and (d) structure of the sample LEDs.
PL decay rates derived from UV and thermal stability tests.

head.ce:title: Salt-embedded carbon nanodots as a UV and thermal stable fluorophore for light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Queensland Micro- and Nano-Technology Centre, Griffith University, Nathan, QLD 4111, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Queensland Micro- and Nano-Technology Centre, Griffith University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental Engineering, Griffith University, QLD 4111, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Physiology, University of Freiburg

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, The University of Queensland, QLD 4072, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering, The University of Queensland

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Queensland Micro- and Nano-Technology Centre, Griffith University, Nathan, QLD 4111, Australia. Tel.: +61 7 3735 7514.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: UV and thermal stable, photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) prepared by embedding CDs in ionic salt crystals such as NaCl, KCl, KBr are demonstrated. The salt crystal embedding matrix does not interfere with CDs strong emission, and provides effective protection to CDs from the environment. The degradation of 20% of the initial luminescence intensity of salt-encapsulated CDs (S-CDs) is 15 times slower under UV and 6 times slower under heat compared to that of CDs in silica matrix. We also demonstrate that the S-CDs can be applied as a color-converting phosphor for typical GaN UV light emitting diodes (LEDs) with significant improvements in stability as well as processability.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent carbon nanodots (CDs) have drawn significant attention as a new member of the nanocarbon family [1,2]. Owing to their bright luminescence, water solubility, biocompatibility, low to nil toxicity, facile synthesis, and low cost, they have been regarded as a viable alternative to semiconductor-based quantum dots [3–6]. Recent years have seen continuous progress made in CDs, including improved quantum yield (QY) [7–9], simplified synthesis methods [10–13], spectral tunability [14] and solid state performance [15]. Promising applications have been demonstrated in biomedical imaging [14,16–18], drug delivery [19], light harvesting [20] and solid state lighting [21–24].

Highly efficient solid state lighting devices such as light emitting diodes (LEDs) can halve the energy consumption of lighting, which currently constitutes 22% of the global power consumption [25]. They are already providing energy savings and environmental benefits in areas like lighting, automobiles, transportation, agriculture, communication and medicine. There are currently two approaches to producing various colored LEDs; one is based on a combination of red, green and blue LEDs, including the use of organic LEDs (OLED). The other popular LED fabrication approach is LED-plus-phosphor, which typically consists of a UV or blue light emitting GaN LED chip combined with coatings of color-converting phosphors. The latter is the cheaper and easier way to produce LEDs of various colors including white, because there is no direct charge carrier injection involved; hence less stringent requirement in fabrication [25]. One of the identified major technological challenges in LEDs is phosphor development. In solid-state lighting, the excitation wavelength is in the range 380–480nm, much longer than that in mercury fluorescent lamps (typically 250nm); therefore, phosphors for traditional fluorescent lamps are not suitable for LEDs and there has been an urgent need to develop new phosphors.

Possessing wide range light absorption with a peak typically at 360nm and a multicolor emission feature, CDs appear to be a promising candidate as an LED phosphor for both LED fabrication approaches. Moreover, CDs do not pose health and safety issues compared to their counterparts, the semiconductor-based quantum dots, such as CdSe, PbS, CdTe, etc. Wang et al. [21] reported for the first time an incorporation of CDs in an OLED structure, and very recently Zhang et al. [24] have demonstrated for the first time that the emission color of CDs-based OLEDs can be controlled by voltage. A few studies have been carried out to employ solid state CDs as the phosphor for UV-blue LEDs, demonstrating its effectiveness in color conversion [22,23]. However, in none of these studies has the long term stability of CDs under UV and heat been investigated.

Since the phosphor׳s UV and thermal stabilities are crucial factors for guaranteeing a longer lifetime of LEDs than traditional incandescent and fluorescent lamps [26,27], it is highly important to investigate how CDs behave under UV and thermal (typically 80°C) irradiations as the typical operating environment in LEDs [28]. Our earlier study [29] has shown that the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of solid state CDs is not stable under either UV or thermal exposure after extended exposure time. Only 20% of initial PL intensity was retained after 250h of UV (4W 365nm) or thermal (80°C) exposure, even when they were homogeneously dispersed in a silicate matrix [29]. The PL degradation may be caused by chemical bond cleavage due to oxidation. Hence, there is a need to find a protective coating for CDs against UV and thermal degradations. For both LED-based and LED-plus-phosphor designs, this coating needs to satisfy the following requirements: (a) does not change PL properties including emission spectra and quantum yield; (b) has suitable refractive index; (c) has good UV and thermal stabilities; (d) transparent in UV and visible ranges. A range of salt crystal materials including NaCl, KCl, and KBr were demonstrated to meet these requirements and can serve as tight embedding matrices that can protect quantum dot nanocrystals from harsh environments [30].

Inspired by its success on quantum dots, herein we apply a salt crystal embedding strategy to CDs and study its effect on protecting CDs from UV and heat degradations. The salt encapsulated CDs (S-CDs) show significant improvement in UV and thermal stabilities, while preserving emission properties from the as-prepared solution sample. Finally the stability enhanced S-CDs were applied as a color converting medium for fabricating blue emitting LEDs.

Organosilane functionalized CDs were synthesized by using N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropylmethyl-dimethoxysilane (AAPMS, Beijing Shenda Fine Chemical, China) as a solvent and citric acid (Chem-Supply, Australia) as a carbon source, following the procedures reported in [6]. The AAPMS was placed in a three-neck flask and heated under N2 reflux. The citric acid was injected when the temperature reached 240°C and held for 1min. The synthesized CDs were mixed with water and filtered through a 0.22μm syringe filter. The filtered CDs were used for further processing including solid film fabrication or embedding with salt crystals.

Thin films of CDs in a silica matrix for use as control samples were fabricated on glass slides by drop casting. 26×15mm glass slides were cleaned by acetone, ethanol and pure water sequentially in an ultrasonic bath. 40μL of silane functionalized CDs was then applied on a clean glass slide. The coated slides were then dried in an oven at 50°C for 5min. The sample solution formed uniform solid films as it contained a large amount of silanols from the synthesis which tend to form bonds with glass substrates or condense with other silanols [15]. The thickness of the solid CD samples (CDs in silica) can be controlled by modifying the amount or concentration of the solution and number of coatings times. Typically 40μL of as-synthesized CDs solution forms a 2μm thick clear film on a 26×15mm glass slide.

The CD solution was mixed with saturated salt solutions (KBr, 217gL−1; KCl, 281gL−1; NaCl, 359gL−1) prepared at a ratio of 1–5 and then the mixture was placed under a HEPA filter controlled environment for at least a week at room temperature for crystallization. The salt crystal-embedded CDs (S-CDs) were washed with saturated solution to remove excess CDs on the surface and dried before milling and sieving. The processed S-CDs were in powder form, which were then fabricated into discs on a 10t press. The S-CD discs were used for optical analyses and stability assessment.

Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using a Thermo Scientific Lumina fluorescence spectrometer and fluorescence lifetimes were recorded using an Edinburgh Photonics FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer with a 372nm pulse laser excitation source. UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured using an Agilent 8453 UV–vis spectrometer. Spectra of the LEDs were recorded by Ocean Optics USB4000 with a fiber optic probe in a closed light chamber. Solid film thickness was measured using a Veeco Dektak 150 surface profiler. Light intensity of the UV lamp was measured by a Newport 1918-C optical power meter. TEM images were captured using a JEOL JEM-2100 electron microscope operating at 200kV.

S-CD discs were used for UV and thermal stability tests. The disc was attached on a glass substrate by using cyanoacrylate glue before being exposed to either UV under a 5W 365nm UV lamp or 80°C oven for its stability assessment. The samples on the glass slides were placed in a quartz cuvette diagonally for PL measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of S-CD-LEDs and the stability test
ce:section.ce:para: LEDs were prepared by mixing 50mg of powdered S-CDs samples with 10mL of commercially available silicone rubber. The mixture was then poured into a transparent mold and a UV LED (3.4V, 100mA) was placed in the mold. Once the LED was secured, the S-CD containing silicone was cured at room temperature for 24h. The prepared LEDs were operated by a variable power supply with the voltage fixed at 3.4V. Light intensities of the samples were measured in a dark chamber equipped with a fiber optic probe.

In order to prevent degradation and increase the solid state CDs’ UV and thermal stabilities, we embed (or encapsulate) CDs in salt matrix because of the structural tightness, optical transparency and UV, thermal stability of salts. The encapsulation can be easily completed by crystallizing out salt from a CD-dispersed saturated salt solution. Three types of crystalline minerals, namely NaCl, KCl and KBr were selected for crystal growth as they all satisfy the aforementioned criteria. In terms of how they perform as a protective embedding matrix for CDs, the crystal lattice structure, in turn the structural compatibility with CDs and the crystal quality may play important roles.

Two weeks after adding CD solution into the prepared saturated salt solution, a large amount of fine crystals were formed along the wall of the container and large size crystals were formed at the bottom of the container ( Fig. 1(a), Fig. S1). The fine crystals harvested from the side walls showed no fluorescence. However the large crystals exhibited blue emission under a UV lamp (365nm), indicating the incorporation of CDs in the salt crystals. This shows that the CDs are too large to be transported by capillary evaporation along the wall. The successful incorporation in the bottom big salt crystals is likely facilitated by the charge interactions between the CD particles and the growing faces of salt crystals [31].

TEM characterization of the S-CDs was challenging because the electron beam can easily damage the embedding matrix of the CDs, i.e., the salt crystals. The TEM images (Fig. 1(b) and (c), Fig. S2) taken from the same spot of the sample under low voltage 200kV show that the salt has been melting and individual CD particles are difficult to identify. The selected area electron diffraction shown in the inset of Fig. 1(d) shows the salt crystal diffraction. Although the CD particles could not be distinctively identified, an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDX) result shown in Fig. 1(e) confirms the existence of CDs in the salt crystal, where the fingerprints of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and silicon from the CDs and signals of potassium and bromide from the salt crystals were all detected.

For spectroscopy measurements and the following stability tests, the S-CDs were first ground into fine powders before being compressed into tablets. The S-CDs in NaCl show blue emission under the UV lamp, with its emission peak around the same wavelength as the CDs embedded in silica as well as CDs in solution, as shown in Fig. 2. The emission spectra of the S-CDs in salt (NaCl, KCl and KBr) are almost identical to each other with peaks appearing around 440nm (Fig. S3(a)). KCl- and KBr-embedded CDs show slight spectrum broadening, which is most likely due to aggregation of CDs during crystallization as the aggregated CDs have higher reabsorption effect than well dispersed CDs [32].

In general, there are no remarkable differences in absorption and PL between the solution and solid-state samples. As shown in Fig. S4, both solution and solid-state CD samples have similar excitation-independent emission peaks. However, with regards to fluorescence (FL) lifetime, CDs in solution have a FL lifetime of 15ns, while both S-CDs in NaCl and CDs embedded in silica show a lifetime of 6.5ns, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The significant shortening of FL lifetime is likely due to the aggregation of CDs in solid or silica matrix [33–35]. It is also observed that S-CDs in KBr and KCl possess a FL lifetime around 5.5ns, slightly shorter than S-CDs in NaCl, which may be attributed to a higher level of aggregation. Even though there are discrepancies in the FL lifetime between solid state and solution dispersion state, the original well-dispersed solution state can be recovered by dissolving S-CDs into water, as indicated by the identical FL lifetime as before being incorporated in salt crystals. This result demonstrates that the salt crystallization process does not change the optical properties of the bare CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV stability of solid state CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Stability to UV irradiation was tested under a 5W 365nm UV lamp for 264h at 200mm from the light source with energy intensity of 30mWcm−2. Direct exposure of CDs in silica to UV resulted in a rapid degradation of PL intensity over the testing period. As shown in Fig. 3, the PL intensity of CDs in silica experienced a fast decay, with about 20% PL loss after 4h of exposure, stabilizing at 10% of the initial intensity after 150h exposure. On the contrary, the S-CD samples showed much improved stability under the same conditions as shown in Fig. 3. The CDs in silica suffered 20% of PL intensity degradation compared to less than 10% PL degradation of all three S-CDs after 4h exposure. S-CDs in NaCl appear to be the most stable specimen; its degradation of 20% of PL initial intensity took 60h which is 15 times slower than that of CDs in silica, and after stabilizing at approximately 200h it retained over 70% of the initial PL intensity level, which is 9 times higher than that of CDs in silica. The high PL stability of NaCl S-CDs exceeds the stability reported on salt crystal embedded CdTe quantum dots [30]. It is also noted that the PL decay rates for the three S-CDs samples were initially similar in the first 4h; however, became considerably different afterwards, with S-CDs in NaCl being the most stable and S-CDs in KBr the least stable. The 30–40% drop of PL intensity is likely caused by oxidation degradation processes due to water and oxygen entrapped in the salt crystals during crystallization. The similar fast rate PL decay at the initial stage in all S-CD samples is probably due to the degradation of the adsorbed CD particles on crystal surface. The following different decay rates in different salts may be related to the salt crystal quality, i.e. the tightness of the salt crystal matrix, and the oxygen diffusion rate within.

The UV decay data over the entire testing time frame can be fitted by a first order exponential decay model, N ( t ) =
N
0
e
− k t
(where N is quantity, k is decay rate and t is time), with good fitting goodness, R
2 =0.98. The k values, i.e. reaction rates, derived from this model are presented in Table 1. This quantitative approximation suggests that by embedding CDs in NaCl, the decay rate has been slowed down by 3-fold compared to CDs in silica.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability of solid state CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CD thermal stability was tested at 80°C, because the temperature of an LED in operation can reach as high as 80°C depending on the DC forward current under ambient conditions [28]. Direct exposure of CDs in silica to the heat showed lower degradation of PL intensity than UV exposure over the testing period of 264h. The degradation of 20% of the initial PL intensity of CDs in silica was reached within less than 1h, while it took approximately 6h for KBr S-CDs, the most thermal-stable specimen, to reach the same degradation level. CDs in silica retained only 20% of the initial PL intensity at the end of the assessment. S-CDs in KBr retained around 80% of the initial PL intensity, which is the best result among the S-CDs tested while S-CDs in NaCl and KCl retained 60% and 40%, respectively, of the initial PL intensity. A very rapid PL intensity degradation in the first 4h of exposure was observed in all samples, followed by a second stage where the degradation became either slower or stabilized. The higher initial decay levels under the heat as compared to those under UV exposure are consistent with the thermal degradation being associated with water and oxygen in the salt crystals. Under heating, the degradation of CDs due to trapped water and oxygen was accelerated. However, after a short while (about 4h) the water was removed from the crystals via evaporation, the degradation of the fluorescence centers was considerably hampered.

With the thermal stability data, only the decays of KBr and NaCl S-CDs can be fitted by the first order exponential decay model, while the KCl S-CDs and CDs in silica require a biexponential decay model, N ( t ) =
N
1
e
−
k
1
t
+
N
2
e
−
k
2
t
, for fitting over the entire testing duration (Fig. S5). The biexponential decay process for the latter group suggests that there may be more than one decay reaction occurring within the systems. Table 1 lists all k values for each specimen. It can be noted that in the biexponential decay process of KCl S-CDs and CDs in silica, between the two ongoing reactions, there is one reaction in dominance (k 1âª¢k 2). Alternatively, the PL decay of KCl S-CDs and CDs in silica can also be described as two-stage first-order reactions with good fitting goodness, with the first 24h as Stage 1 and after 24h as Stage 2. The reaction rate in Stage 1 is more than 50 times faster than Stage 2, as k 1 value suggests. Overall, again NaCl S-CDs show the mildest decay rate according to the model.

The grounded S-CD powders were mixed in a silicon rubber, and mixture was then molded over a GaN deep-blue LED as the color-converting medium. Emission spectra of the S-CD-LEDs are shown in Fig. 4. The S-CD-LEDs exhibit red-shifted blue emission (490nm) from the bare UV-LEDs (400nm), and each S-CD-LED shows different levels of emission intensity. In contrast, the LED produced with CDs in silica has very weak emission, which was mainly caused by experimental difficulties in the LED manufacturing process including, obtaining dispersible powders, mixing and curing within the silicone rubber. However, there were no complications caused by using S-CDs for the LED fabrication because of the improved processability by being incorporated in the salt matrix. As shown in Fig. 4(a), each sample LED has noticeable differences in PL intensity from the UV LED. The intensity of emission peak at 450nm suggests that NaCl salt has incorporated more CDs in the crystal than KCl or KBr since the amount of S-CDs used for each LED was kept exactly the same as well as other operating conditions. The amount of CDs incorporated in the salt crystals may be directly associated with salt crystallization rate. Among the three salts, NaCl has the slowest crystallization rate while KCl the fastest.

It is worth noting that the concentration of phosphor coating can be used to adjust the color of the LED. By doubling the amount of S-CDs in the LEDs from 50mg to 100mg, the emission can be controlled from blue to greenish blue (NaCl S-CDs’ CIE coordinate (x, y) shifted from 0.2218, 0.3796 to 0.2426, 0.4337) as shown in Fig. 4(b).

The prepared LEDs were operated continuously for one week to assess the CDs stability under the tangible operating conditions in the LEDs. As expected from the stability assessment results, NaCl S-CD-LED showed a reasonable stability level at 77% of PL intensity retention while the other S-CD-LEDs and the LED made from CDs in silica lost almost all their PL emission after a week of operation (Fig. 4(c), Fig. S6). The stability level of NaCl S-CD-LED showed a significant improvement over CDs in silica-LED, which confirms that salt encapsulation has transformed CDs into a UV and thermal stable color-converting phosphors for UV LEDs.

CDs provide a promising alternative to heavy-metal containing quantum dots as an environment friendly and low cost phosphor component for LEDs. However, CDs without any protections tend to degrade under UV and thermal exposures due to oxidation processes, as reflected by their quantum yields. We have demonstrated that salt crystal embedding strategy provides an effective and versatile method to protect CDs from UV and thermal degradations without compromising optical properties, which is highly important in applications in solid state lighting. Our study shows that NaCl matrix renders the best UV protection to CDs with 70% retention of initial PL intensity after 250h UV exposure, while KBr crystals provide the best protection against thermal protection with about 80% PL retention after being heated under 80°C for 250h. In comparison to CDs in silica, the PL degradation rate of CDs was slowed down by 15 times under UV and 6 times under heat by being embedded in salt matrices. By incorporating them into commercial UV LEDs and operating over a week, we have demonstrated that salt-embedded CDs feature significantly improved PL stability as well as improved processability as a color-converting phosphor. The methodology demonstrated here may pave the way for wider applications of CDs in solid state optics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Centre for Microscopy and Microanalysis at the University of Queensland for providing training and access to the electron microscopes. THK acknowledges the support of an Australian Postgraduate Award and Queensland Smart Futures PhD Scholarship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2014.04.002.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J. Phys. Chem. C Nanomater. Interfaces

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: J.Y. Tsao, in: Proceedings of the Conference on Lasers Elecro-Optics, Baltimore, 2005, 1–3, 143.
